summary: Designed by Ralf Groesel and 5x World Champion Aaron Hadlow. The Peak is Harlem's 5-strut big air machine built for explosive vertical lift, insane hangtime, and top-end control. PRoWELD LE, Twincross Flylite canopy, Mylar struts, fixed pulley bridle with one-sided stopper. 2025 Big Air Champion Lorenzo Casati's kite of choice. Jason Montreal's #3 best big air kite of 2025-26.
best_for: Big air riders chasing record-breaking height

sources.summary: Aaron Hadlow's fingerprints are all over this kite. The hangtime is genuinely special and the fixed pulley bridle gives exceptional direct steering when overpowered. Deserves to be mentioned alongside the Rebel and Orbit.
Jason Montreal put it at #3 for a reason. The Peak is punching way above its brand profile. Boutique feel with world-class big air performance.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 20 - Harlem Peak
reviews.excerpt: and welcome to another plus-sized kite review, where I wrap up a backto backto back trilogy of Brainchild reviews with the latest from Harlem, the new Peak. And I'll also cover some thoughts on their sick looking new board, the Ascent towards the end of the video. A big thanks to Harlem pro for prov
reviews.verdict: in summary, if you are a very top level rider looking for the absolute maximum height specifically and hand time possible from a kite that you can do, although this is going to be getting right up there, it might be just slightly behind the top performers in the field like the Rebel DAB or Slingshot NXT, but for the rest of us mortals, the slight loss you might have in boosting in hang time is more than likely made up for with the ease of use and the overall friendlier riding experience this kit
reviews.full_transcript: Hello, and welcome to another plus-sized kite review, where I wrap up a backto backto back trilogy of Brainchild reviews with the latest from Harlem, the new Peak. And I'll also cover some thoughts on their sick looking new board, the Ascent towards the end of the video. A big thanks to Harlem pro for providing this demo gear. So, be sure to hit up their website if you are wanting to get yours right now or keep an eye out for them and other Harlem gear as always through Mac Kiteboarding. I will provide some links to both of these options in the description as well as some videos about the more technical aspects of the Brainchild company and this specific kit's design. in the event you'd like to know more about that side of things because as usual, I'll mainly be focusing on the experience of riding the kite and where I think it sits in the big air market for areas like boosting and looping, etc. If you haven't seen my previous videos, go back and check out other reviews on kites like the Harlem Thrive, for example. As per usual with these reviews, my jumps and trick heights have been measured using the surfer app for its across the board consistency. Uh be it in loops, boosts, board offs, or rotations. So, if you want to start getting some consistency in your jump measuring for progression across different styles of jumps, definitely give them a check out. With all of that out of the way, let's uh get on to one of the most exciting kites on the market right now, the Harlem Peak. Just like they did with the Harlem Thrive review last year, Harlem were gracious enough to send me both a 9 and 12 m demo size to give a blast. And I have to say, first off, that I love their confidence because right away that tells me they're very happy with the results across the board uh of sizes with this kite. And to me, it's always a bonus to get to see how a particular kite will perform in different sizes. Not to mention, it makes it a lot easier for me to get good footage because we don't exactly always have 9 m strength wind here all the time. Um, I don't get the opportunity with many reviews because frankly I'm a long way away from any brands except for North. So, it's really hard to get kites other than just a single size down here. That's why I'm always reviewing nines. Right out of the bag, as I have now experienced with each brainchild kite, the material has a unique feel to it compared to other more dense and heavier traditional Dacron kites. When you infl them, just like the lighter Alula model kites, you have to be rather careful not to just leave them sitting down on the beach without anything on top of them, or you risk them going on a little journey of their own. As you walk out to your launching area or just move the inflated kite around, you'll likely notice that there is a little more give and flex in the frame in the stronger wind compared to an Alula kite. And this is something you will notice when you're riding in the stronger and gustier winds as well. But I'll go into that a little bit later and how it affects the kite's performance once the kite is up in the air. Uh, again, it's going to be one of the lighter kites out there, if not the lightest you've probably ever tried. But of the specific brainchild kites, I'd say this is actually the heavier of them, making it somewhere in line with a lighter to light medium weighted uh, three strut dachron kite or a mediumweed Alulia kite for the most part. So, similar, but perhaps slightly heavier than say Trigger, um, but still lighter than most of the bigger kites on the market. As you may already be aware, the kite's shape and style is very similar to that of the Duo Tone Rebel, and there is a lot of that similar DNA flowing through the kite in its characteristics through the air. So, if you haven't already seen my 2025 Rebel SLS or DAB reviews, you may want to check those out because I will likely do a bit of comparison talk from those kites to this one over the course of this video. I have not unfortunately ever tried Rebels in sizes larger than 9 m, so anything relating to larger sizes is just going to be speculation or feedback from other riders. I was able to get out the 12 m first. So, let's start there with my first impressions in the lighter wind sessions. Uh, I found that like the Rebel SLS and most five strut kites, to be fair, this kite wasn't the best in the low-end department, uh, when compared to the most impressive threest strut Alula kites, but it was fairly impressive for a nonula model. Over both sizes of the kite being a big air focus model, it should come as no surprise that the most fun I had on either size when I was fairly well powered, which meant over 20 knots in the 12 m and over 25 knots in the 9 m size. Now, of course, low end is always a little hard to evaluate with kites, but I did find when riding back to back on this 12 m Peak and my 12 m Evo in the lightest conditions, I tended to just be a bit more powered and able to do a little bit more get upwind a bit easier on my EVO DAB. It was when the wind started to get up to at least the 18 to 20 knot mark that I was actually finding in terms of boosting height and hang time. The peak was very comparable to my Evo. The bar pressure was also really close on both kites, but you could feel more consistent power with the Evo, particularly in the lulls of the wind. when you would engage with the bar to move the kite around. As you would expect, the Evo was a bit more direct as well and quicker to engage due to the lack of its pulleys on the wing tips like with the Peak. Obviously, I am far more used to this Evo after 2 years on the kite. So, I found it easier to keep track of it and time most of my jumps, but it didn't take me too long to get the hang of the peaks timing and slight response delay. and I really had quite a blast on it in the sessions that I was riding it, even to a point where I built up the confidence to throw a few loops on this 12 m. Although, I must admit, I was being a bit less reckless than I would usually be on my Evo. And I waited until I was sure that I was going to be jumping at least over 8 to 9 m before pulling the trigger on any loops. I think I could have gotten away with a little less height to loop this 12, but perhaps not quite as little as the Evo and certainly not as much as the Harlem Thrive, which you can see me looping this clip right here. In any case, I can't imagine having a 12 m these days that I wouldn't feel confident looping when the kites like these are out there on the market. With both the Peak and Thrive, you have to consider to some extent that I'm mainly comparing them to what I still consider the best overall 12 meter kite I've ever flown in my Evo Dab. So, this is very rare company and both kites are riding beautifully in this size. Even if you aren't into looping big kites, having a kite in this size that is still fairly light on the bar, but gives solid performance and the ability to function even when you start to find yourself overpowered. And it's just a wonderful thing for progression if you ask me. Outside of just setting surfer PBS or doing bigger loops for progression everywhere else from rotations to board offs to consistency in jumping in both directions, a higher performance larger sized bigger kite like these Harlem kites that you feel confident on is about as essential as having a 12 m freestyle kite was 5 to 10 years ago. If I have to pick between these three 12 m kites, I think I would lean towards my uh current EVO DAB, mainly because of the low end and my size. Um, but between the two Harlems, in a more powered situation, I'd go for the Peak and in a situation where I'm riding in more lighter winds, I'd lean towards the Thrive probably. Um, it is a little bit more direct on the bar and I think the low end is probably a little bit better because it's three strut, but it um certainly is worth considering. The peak obviously if you're more into higher jumps and more hang time and it would certainly be use more useful there and also in a gustier place that the wind can ramp up unexpectedly. It's hard to imagine many, if any, straight Dacaron kites keeping up with any of these three. And I'd wager that only a few of the top Alula kites will be in a similar category for overall performance in the 12 m size. Kites like perhaps the Slingshot NXT. Um perhaps the Ozone Vortex, maybe even the Rebel DLAB. Um but that would be a heavier kite. Now, having an excellent opening experience with the 12 m, let's get on to where things really get exciting and our time spent on the 9 m in much stronger winds comparing it to a lot more kites. So, first impressions, like with each brainchild kite, were certainly how impressive the ease of use when piling the kite around the window is in terms of bar pressure. The general flight characteristics did remind me, as I already mentioned, of a Rebel, but some new kind of Rebel that is a bit faster to act. It's a bit lighter on the bar, uh, even more so than the SLS, and it's faster to respond. I can't really understate how unique the Brainchild kites all feel when compared to most other non-ala kites on the market. Despite how fast they are with that light bar pressure and rapid response time from bar to kite, they are particularly easy to use, at least once you get used to the speed of course at which they travel. And every one of my friends who tests these kites with me do all love the feeling. And they tend to be the kites they are most interested in flying again and again. And it's always hard going back to a standard Dacron kite after them. One knock still, however, with these kites is that when the wind really cranks or gets a bit gusty, they do still tend to wobble and flap a bit more than your top Alula kites and potentially a bit more than some of the top traditional Dacron five strut kites. This habit of the kites uh wobbling in the wind can be distracting. Although, as I mentioned in the other reviews, I didn't find with the other malls it impacted the performance of the kite too much because of how fast they were and how direct the feeling was. With the Peak, the kite itself was less impacted by this tendency to wobble than the three strut brainchild kites. But one thing I did notice on a few occasions was that in conjunction with the pulleys on the wing tips, the kite's flapping could make it a bit harder to track the kite at times as it flowed through the air, particularly compared to the classic rebels or the trigger when I rode them all back to back. I'll try to go into a little bit more detail on this phenomenon when I discuss the looping aspect of the kite because it was mainly when the kite was passing over my head as I was coming down from big jumps or with loops with the heloops that I noticed it the most. We rode this 9 m peak on several different occasions in wind from around 22 to 35 knots at times between the range of kites I'm currently testing which you will see in upcoming videos and of course against some of the recent kites I've tested like the Trigger Hyper model or the Rebels. And in each session, it was always one of the top two boosters and one of the harder kites to get off of because of how much fun it was to ride while the wind was pumping. The other guys I test with were always more inclined to stay on this kite for as long as possible because of how much they were enjoying it each time. So, let's get on to the key categories, starting with boosting and hang time. As soon as I drop the word rebel and any reference to there being similarities, you have to know the kite I'm describing is going to have some serious sheet and go power. And this peak is no exception. Despite its friendlier feeling and nature through the bar, you can really feel the power build up to rocket you off the water when you do dig your edge in, load up the lines, and get ready to blast off. The kite is very good at allowing you to hold down a ton of power while maintaining a confidence inducing level of control while you're riding along the water and building your line tension before you send it to 12. What is also a bit unique, at least that we found, is that perhaps more than any other kite we've tested, it has an uncanny gift of assisting you to jump ridiculously vertically even when you're not timing it the best or using the perfect technique. This habit of the kite getting up above your head quickly and yanking you off the water very vertically does make it harder to assess if the kite itself is boosting you higher or it's making it easier for you to boost higher by way of your jump trajectory. But either way, you get a lot of height out of it with almost every launch. Like the Rebels, it is very, very generous with its sweet spot for boosting when compared to the rest of the market. So riders of most levels are going to benefit from that, especially in the more challenging conditions, be it chop or inconsistent wind. Of the brainchild kites I've tested so far, it does appear to be the best overall booster with the most easily accessible hang time in that you don't have to be as mindful about how you pilot the kite overhead to get the benefit of added lift and hang time as you descend. But with that said, I still don't think it quite rivals the top few Alula kites like the Evo DAB or Rebel DAB or perhaps Slingshot NXT for pure lift and hang time. Although you do get rocketed off the water so vertically, it doesn't quite feel like it's keeping up with that top tier of boosters. Um although it doesn't feel very far off at all from the top uh five strut dachron cuts like the Rebel SLS for example. Um, to be honest though, it is of course always tricky to evaluate because the right gusts come and go in every big air session. But having rode the kite for a number of hours now back to back with a variety of kites, um, it is always one of the highest boosting. Um, and so that was the overall impression we got. There certainly is an argument that because the kite is so good at getting you off the water, unless you are a top level rider, you might be likely to overpilot it and lose its position more easily than the slower moving Rebels or NXT. But if you go back to that Red review, I believe I mentioned that although I felt like the Evo Dab had the potential to go higher, it it's more likely most people would get considerably um consistently higher on the Rebel uh SLS simply because it's not as fast and the sweet spot is so large. So, you could make a little similar argument here that the rebels are going to be easier to jump more consistently higher than the peak just cuz they're not moving as fast. With all things considered, I still think this is definitely one of the better boosters on the market, just slightly behind the top ulcites for overall boosting and hang time potential. So, moving on to looping from both lightly powered loops to fullbore mega loops. And then when doing heli loops as you descend. This is again one of the nicer kites out there for loops when you consider how much power it has as well. The looping motion of the kite is quite tight but still a bit fuller um and ever so slightly deep here with more power than the other brainchild kites. Though this is probably not the best option of them for doubles. But the kite is still so light and fast that doesn't create the same intimidating loop as things like the Rebel or the XR or some of the Yankee pulling kites in the larger size. Because of that slight delay through the pulleys, it did take a session to get up the card for me to send that 12 m around some powered loops. And there certainly was a moment of held breath, but the kite is so smooth and still fast enough. It did the job with relative ease. The loops on each sides of this kite are fairly predictable and quite consistent in different winds, be it gusty or patchy, as well as strong with a lot of power in the kite. Although all of us are seasoned loopers, we do still try to loop these kites from a variety of heights, both on our strong and weaker sides, often throwing some rotations into the mix, as you can see here. And I don't think any of us had too many instances where the kite struggled to cleanly get around the loop and quickly progress back up towards a safe catching position. As I mentioned earlier though, I did find when in the gustier winds in particular, there were times when either the slight delay from the pulleys on the wing tips in conjunction with the wobbliness of the kite or perhaps just the kite's flight pattern did mean on a handful occasions I felt like I lost control of the position of the kite overhead. As you can see in these clips, even more so when I was doing one-handed loops. You have to also consider with testing, I'm often trying to put the kite in a precarious position, especially if I think the kite's going to bail me out. This momentary loss of tracking for me would also likely be a little bit to do with the kite's tendency to fly back up overhead um to create more lift. The same way it does when you are leaving the water with your jumps. It didn't happen very often in the a whole bunch of sessions and I'm sure more time on the water getting used to the kite would uh take a lot of that um unfamiliarity out of it. But of the brainchild kites, this was the only one where I found that to happen. So I do associate it a bit with the floaty nature when combined with the pulleys which I am not as used to. I haven't really noticed the same problems happen to me on traditional Rebels during their testing. However, I mentioned in that review that I did sometimes have an issue with the Rebels and even my Evos on occasion where when you pull the loop too tightly, they can often pause before they go to climb back up overhead. Usually from putting the kite in a bad position, either too low or to the side of the window. And cranking the bar too much can do it as well. Now, with that very easy to handle power, top tier hang time, and very accessible looping style, it's probably no surprise that one of the better attributes of this kite, like all the Brainchild kites I tend to find, is that they are quite confidence inspiring. due to the boosting prowess and slightly less direct feedback um to say the thrive or the trigger in particular. I would put this peak somewhere slightly behind those for looping specific progression. Although it is still very good for that when compared to the rest of the industry. However, when it comes to trying to push big jumps, go higher, throw board offs or controlled rotations into the equation as well as loops, this is certainly one of the best kites on the market to help you push your limits. The combination of all those variables, despite its tendency to wobble a little bit in challenging wind, do make it for me one of the better kites to still feel that confidence when the conditions get more and more challenging. And I think for a lot of riders that is going to translate very nicely to pushing their own skills to another level, particularly with jumping higher and pulling the trigger on bigger tricks and bigger loops as they get to those new heights. An edge this Peak might also have on the other brainchild kites in the smaller sizes at least is that because it's not quite as fast and directly responsive is that it will likely be more forgiving to progressing riders who aren't quite ready to handle the crazy speed of some of the other kites. And even more advanced riders of course will still have the ability just need to take a little bit more time to adapt their timing to it. In either case, unless you are already coming from a top level Alula kite or another brainchild kite, you're very likely to be blown away by the level of accessible performance flowing through the lines from this kite. for the scores. Well, about as high and nicely balanced across the board as you can get and as you might expect from one of the most enjoyable kites we've had the pleasure to review so far. So in summary, if you are a very top level rider looking for the absolute maximum height specifically and hand time possible from a kite that you can do, although this is going to be getting right up there, it might be just slightly behind the top performers in the field like the Rebel DAB or Slingshot NXT, but for the rest of us mortals, the slight loss you might have in boosting in hang time is more than likely made up for with the ease of use and the overall friendlier riding experience this kite provides, especially in stronger conditions to build your confidence. I would say though that if I was looking for a full quiver of Harlem kites, I think my preferences would be to lean to the Peak in sizes 7 8 9 um then likely move over to the Thrive in sizes 10 and up where the kite, although it may lack a little power, I think it would make up for it with speed and potentially low end and certainly looping in a bigger size. And of course, if I was going to go for a six, which I'm not interested in, I'd probably also go for the Thrive, um, if I wanted to do doubles, but you're not really losing at all with any size for all of these kites. Now, before I finish up this review, I'll also just throw in some thoughts on the new Harlem Ascent Board as well, because I rode it a few times over a number of sessions between these two kites. You will notice I had to swap the foot straps over to some north ones, mainly because I just couldn't comfortably fit into the Harlem ones. Sadly, although they were long enough and high enough, they were too narrow for my wide, flat feet. So, although I could just squeeze into them, they certainly weren't the most comfortable for my feet, nor were they easy enough to get off when it came to board offs. The North Straps are also a fair bit heavier than the Harlem ones. So, as a bigger rider, I actually enjoyed the little bit of extra weight they add to the board. To get this super light board, a little bit closer to what I'm used to really, but I would imagine a lighter rider with narrower feet would just love these super light, comfy Harlem straps. When out on the water riding the boards, the ascent reminded me of some kind of tasty mix of the Lua Falcon with a touch of the older model Imperator and a little dash of my Atmas carbon in that it was very light, very fast, but didn't require much um or at least as much rear heelside pressure to move it around like I feel when I ride my uh Hime, for example. Harlem's team, the and the board's designer, Ralph Groel, talk about how the board was specifically designed to work with the characteristics of the Peak. But I can't pretend I'm in a level to really feel or understand that. But I can say that in terms of how it rode, um, I like that it felt like it was better suited to a more even weight distribution. Um, and when I was on it, it felt like it was benefiting me when I used a more square base evenly balanced over the full board. The way I tend to ride with my atmos um, and the ray I am more confident in stronger winds. I found this balanced stance usually means for me that I can hold more power when I'm lit and it also helps me jump more vertically, more consistently. something I can struggle with a bit on my Haime board as I discussed in that older board review. To be fair, of course, to the Haime board, it is a 139x 42, whereas the Atmas I'm more used to and preferring stronger winds is a 138 just like the Ascent. Whereas the Atmas in terms of width is 41 and the Harlem even thinner at a trim 40 wide. Even with the slight size difference, the Haimey does just feel like it rides like a much bigger board of the three of these. So, you will notice in some of the clips uh where I'm riding, usually in the lighter winds where I need a little more board under me to stay powered up, I'll be on the high me and when the things start to get stronger, I'll be on one of the other two boards. I found I lost a little bit of power um in comparison to the larger size Himey when switching back to the Ascent to a tune of it feeling like it just needed an extra 2 or three knots more wind for someone as heavy as I am to get the most out of it. But given its size and the focus of its design being extreme big air, it totally makes sense cuz that's where I really enjoyed using it. In the stronger wind sessions where I liked it the most, although the speed of the board is noticeably more than my Atmas, it felt really easy to adapt to and control with very little time on it. The weight of it made it great for board offs, and the unique shape was a delight to land on when coming in pretty hot. Usually with smaller feeling boards, I find I tend to lose control a little bit coming in hot from big loops or with bad timing, but that wasn't really the case with this board. And I have to say I really did enjoy feeling maxed out in big air on it. Finally, I should also add the bar has become an industry favorite now between my friends and I, not only for high Y bars, but really of the bars out there. It's nice and simple, which I like, and has got the cleanest auto unwind. Still, after a year of riding it, it's not really binding up, and it is among the most direct bars in terms of overall feel. So, there you have it. Another huge review and more excellent products from this brand. If you managed to make it this far, a massive thank you to you. And please, as always, like and subscribe and leave me a comment of your thoughts of the review of the kite or anything else to do with kiting and the kites you'd like me to test. Follow me on Instagram if you want to stay up tod date with what I'm kiting next because there's quite a few different kites in production at the moment and I'd love to hear your thoughts on them. So until next time, goodbye. [Music]
